user_goals_summary: The user writes donor-facing communications that center community agency and treat donors as strategic co-architects, not charitable givers. They value emotional resonance achieved through concrete, scene-level detail rather than sentiment, and they insist on narrative structures where individual stories anchor data and asks loop back to specific people.

rubric.name: Agency-Centered Framing
rubric.description: Community members and donors are the grammatical and narrative subjects — acting, choosing, building. The organization appears only in a supporting role. Default sentence structure should make people the ones doing things, not having things done for or to them.

dimensions.label: Community members and beneficiaries are the grammatical subjects of key sentences
Verbs describe what people chose, did, or built — not what was provided, delivered, or given to them
The organization appears in a supporting or enabling role, never as the primary actor driving the narrative
Local partners are credited for their own initiative, trust-building, and groundwork — not positioned as intermediaries
No language of 'delivering' outcomes, 'bringing' services, or framing beneficiaries as passive recipients

rubric.name: Scene-Level Specificity
rubric.description: Emotional resonance comes from concrete, physical, observable detail — not adjectives or sentiment. A person's dignity is shown through what they did, carried, decided, or said, never explained in abstract terms. Details do the work that language shouldn't have to.

dimensions.label: Key narrative moments are grounded in a specific place, time, and physical action
Character and agency are shown through action and detail, not described through adjectives or characterization
At least one specific, observable detail per featured person does the emotional work (what they carried, where they walked, what they did the night before)
Quotes reflect the speaker's own experience and decisions, not commentary about the program or organization

rubric.name: Narrative Through-Line
rubric.description: The piece is built around one anchoring person whose story opens the piece, gives data its emotional context, and connects to the closing ask. Sections build on each other sequentially — data lands as 'she wasn't alone,' and the ask continues the story rather than pivoting away from it.

dimensions.label: Opens with a specific person making a specific choice — before any programmatic or organizational context
Data and metrics are introduced as extensions of the anchoring story, not as a standalone section
The closing ask connects directly back to the opening person or someone in their story
Each section builds on the one before it — no section reads as a standalone block disconnected from the narrative

rubric.name: Donor as Strategic Co-Architect
rubric.description: Donors are framed as people who made strategic decisions that produced specific, causal outcomes — not people being asked to fill a gap or respond to need. Past contributions are named as bets on a model that produced measurable results; new asks are positioned as the next decision in an ongoing strategy.

dimensions.label: Past donor decisions are named as producing specific, causal outcomes — not thanked in vague or generic terms
Uses language of decisions, bets, models, and investment — not need, gaps, generosity, or charity
New ask is positioned as a continuation of a strategy already in motion, not a new or separate request
Specific funding amounts are tied to specific, concrete outcomes or unlocks — not abstract impact

rubric.name: Emotionally Honest Tone
rubric.description: The piece is emotionally resonant without being heavy-handed. Early or incomplete data is framed honestly without overpromising. Urgency comes from real, concrete scarcity — real waitlists, real numbers — not manufactured sentiment or inflated language.

dimensions.label: No heightened emotional language, superlatives, or adjectives doing work that concrete details should do
Early or partial data is framed honestly — acknowledged as early without undermining or overpromising
Urgency comes from real numbers (waitlists, cohort sizes, timelines), not abstract appeals or manufactured pressure
Emotional moments are earned through accumulated specific detail, not asserted through tone or rhetoric

coaching_notes: Agency framing is this user's non-negotiable — flag any sentence where the organization is the primary actor or where a community member is a passive recipient. They think structurally about narrative: every section must connect to the anchoring story, and standalone data or disconnected asks will be rejected. When providing templates or drafts, include fully realized illustrative sentences that model the right register and sentence-level craft — this user wants to see what the sentence should *do*, not just where content should go.

user_goals_summary: The user writes donor-facing communications (newsletters, impact updates, appeals) that center community members and donors as active agents in a shared strategy. They prioritize scene-level specificity and narrative structure over sentiment, and they want every piece to feel honest about its purpose — whether it's a full ask, a check-in, or something in between.

coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: the organization never occupies the hero position, especially at the close — this is the single most consistent correction across iterations. The user thinks in terms of concrete parallel details: if the story shows someone commuting forty minutes, the ask should echo that specific image for the next person. When the broad framework is right, feedback becomes highly surgical — focus on structural seams (story-to-data transitions, story-to-ask transitions) as the places where framing is most likely to break down.

rubric.name: Agency-Centered Framing
rubric.description: Community members and donors are the grammatical and narrative subjects throughout — acting, choosing, building. The organization appears only in a supporting role. Verbs must be decisive and transitive ('enrolled,' 'completed,' 'launched') rather than soft or passive-adjacent ('spent time building,' 'came in with'). This standard holds through the close and into the ask.

dimensions.label: Community members and beneficiaries are the grammatical subjects of key sentences

coaching_notes: This user locks sections surgically once they're working and iterates on specific weak points — never ask for a wholesale revision when a targeted fix is needed. Their deepest non-negotiable is grammatical: the subject-verb structure of every sentence carries philosophical weight about who has agency, and 'soft-active' constructions (spent time, came in with) fail the same test as passive voice. When they say a draft 'isn't quite there,' ask which specific element is off before revising — they think in precise editorial terms and can usually name the problem in one sentence.

user_goals_summary: Writes donor-facing pieces (newsletters, appeals, check-ins) that center community members as the agents of their own stories while positioning donors as strategic co-architects of measurable outcomes. Values concrete, scene-level detail over sentiment, and insists on narrative through-lines where the ask grows directly out of the anchoring person's story rather than pivoting to a separate fundraising register.

rubric.description: The piece is emotionally resonant without being heavy-handed. Emotional moments are earned through accumulated concrete detail, not asserted through tone. Urgency comes from real, concrete scarcity — specific waitlist numbers, cohort sizes, timelines — not manufactured sentiment or guilt. The piece is transparent about what it is and what it's asking for.

dimensions.label: No heightened emotional language, superlatives, or adjectives doing work that concrete details should do
Early or partial data is acknowledged as early without undermining or overpromising
Urgency comes from real numbers (waitlists, cohort sizes, timelines, specific funding gaps), not abstract appeals or manufactured pressure
Emotional moments are earned through accumulated specific detail — the closing earns its weight without guilt-tripping or pushing
The piece is upfront about its purpose — whether it's a check-in, an ask, or a bridge to a future ask — rather than disguising intent

coaching_notes: Non-negotiables: the organization never occupies center stage, donors are strategic partners not charitable givers, and specific nouns always beat categories. This user will revise verb choice and grammatical subjects before anything else — sentence-level agency framing is the foundation all other criteria rest on. When torn between emotional polish and structural integrity, this user chooses structure every time — they'll accept a draft with brackets before they'll accept one with the wrong framing.

writing_type: nonprofit donor communications (newsletters, appeals, impact reports, companion emails)
user_goals_summary: Writes donor-facing pieces that center community members as agents of their own change, position donors as strategic co-architects of a proven model, and build emotional resonance through concrete, physically observable detail rather than sentiment. Values a single anchoring story that carries through to a specific, story-connected ask — where data, donor framing, and the close all feel like extensions of one narrative rather than separate sections.

rubric.name: Agency-Centered Framing
rubric.description: Community members and donors are the grammatical and narrative subjects throughout — acting, choosing, building. Participants are shown as having existing plans, competence, and momentum before any program involvement. The organization appears only in a supporting role. Verbs are decisive and transitive ('enrolled,' 'completed,' 'launched,' 'opened,' 'signed,' 'hired') rather than soft or passive-adjacent ('spent time building,' 'came in with,' 'needed a room'). This framing holds through the close and into the ask.

dimensions.label: Community members are the grammatical subjects of key sentences, shown as having existing knowledge, plans, and momentum before any program involvement
Verbs are decisive and transitive — enrolled, completed, launched, opened, signed, hired — not soft-active constructions like 'spent time building,' 'came in with,' or 'needed'
The organization appears in a supporting or enabling role — no language of 'delivering,' 'bringing,' or driving outcomes; no savior framing
Local partners are credited for their own initiative, trust-building, and groundwork — positioned as trust-builders with their own agency, not intermediaries or logistics
Final sentences remain with community members or their actions — the organization does not reclaim the narrative voice at the close

rubric.name: Donor as Strategic Co-Architect
rubric.description: Donors are framed as people who made strategic decisions that produced specific, causal outcomes — not people being asked to fill a gap or respond to need. Past contributions are named as bets on a model or mechanism that produced measurable results; new asks are positioned as the next decision in an ongoing strategy. Funding amounts unlock participant milestones and outcomes, not registrations, enrollments, or program deliverables.

dimensions.label: Past donor decisions are named as producing specific, causal outcomes — not thanked in vague or generic terms
Uses language of decisions, bets, models, mechanisms, and investment — not need, gaps, generosity, or charity
New ask is positioned as a continuation of a strategy already in motion, not a new or separate request
Specific funding amounts are tied to participant outcome actions (complete certification, open stall, sign contract, hire staff) — not registrations, enrollments, or program deliverables

rubric.name: Narrative Through-Line
rubric.description: The piece is built around one anchoring person whose story opens the piece, gives data its emotional context, and connects to the closing ask. Data lands as 'she wasn't alone.' The ask carries forward the anchoring person's specific verb and noun patterns — their grammar — so the reader feels the tiers as a continuation of the story rather than a pivot. The echo is embodied in matching verbs and nouns, not pointed at with bridge sentences. The ask extends the story forward through specific people connected to the anchoring person — their neighbors, their waitlisted peers, their next cohort — so the ask feels like the story's next chapter.

dimensions.label: Opens with a specific person making a specific choice — before any programmatic or organizational context
Data and metrics are introduced as extensions of the anchoring story ('she wasn't alone'), not as a standalone section

The ask carries forward the anchoring person's specific actions and milestones through matching verb/noun echoes — not by naming the connection ('the same move she made') or using bridge sentences alone
Each section builds on the one before it — no section reads as a standalone block disconnected from the narrative

The ask connects forward through specific people tied to the anchoring person (their neighbors, people they mentored, people on their waitlist) so the next cohort feels like a continuation of the story, not a separate pitch

rubric.name: Scene-Level Specificity
rubric.description: Emotional resonance comes from concrete, physical, observable detail — not adjectives or sentiment. A person's dignity is shown through what they did, carried, decided, or already knew — the detail does the 'dignity work.' Nouns name specific things (a food handler's certification, a market stall, a vendor contract, LLC paperwork) rather than categories (a certification, a milestone, a resource). Details are physically observable — the kind a camera could capture.

dimensions.label: Key narrative moments are grounded in a specific place, time, and physical action — a kitchen table, a cooler, a morning enrollment line
Character and agency are shown through action and physical detail, not described through adjectives or characterization
At least one specific, physically observable detail per featured person does the emotional work (what they carried, where they walked, what they prepared, what they already knew)

Nouns name specific things — 'food handler's certification,' 'market stall,' 'vendor contract,' 'LLC paperwork,' 'expansion map' — not categories like 'a certification,' 'a milestone,' or 'a resource'
Quotes reflect the speaker's own experience and decisions in their own voice, not commentary about the program or organization

rubric.name: Emotionally Honest Tone
rubric.description: The piece is emotionally resonant without being heavy-handed. Emotional moments are earned through accumulated concrete detail, not asserted through tone. Urgency comes from real, concrete scarcity — specific waitlist numbers, cohort sizes, timelines — not manufactured sentiment or guilt. The piece is transparent about what it is and what it's asking for.

dimensions.label: No heightened emotional language, superlatives, or adjectives doing work that concrete details should do
Early or partial data is acknowledged as early without undermining or overpromising
Urgency comes from real numbers (waitlists, cohort sizes, timelines, specific funding gaps), not abstract appeals or manufactured pressure
Emotional moments are earned through accumulated specific detail — the closing earns its weight without guilt-tripping or pushing
The piece is upfront about its purpose — whether it's a check-in, an ask, or a bridge to a future ask — rather than disguising intent

coaching_notes: Non-negotiables: community members as grammatical agents with decisive verbs; donors framed as strategic co-architects, never gap-fillers; specific nouns over categories in every case (this user will iterate indefinitely until nouns are real objects). Preferred trade-off: will accept placeholders and slower progress over any invented or fabricated detail — accuracy of real people's stories is a hard constraint, and the user trusts structure-with-brackets over polished-but-false. The user thinks in terms of story architecture: every section must earn its place by building on the one before it, and the ask must feel like the story's next chapter, not a fundraising appendix.
